The market is calmer than it looks

VIX is at 12, credit spreads tightened again last week, and rates vol is back to 2019 levels. Here's what that's actually telling us — and what it isn't.

Eli Rosenfeld · Feb 9, 2026

Subscribe to get every issue

Three things happened in the last ten trading sessions that, taken together, are doing more to compress volatility than anything the Fed said in January. They're worth walking through, because the takeaway is not the obvious one.

One: the dispersion trade is over

For most of 2025, the difference between single-stock vol and index vol — what traders call "dispersion" — was historically wide. That meant the market was paying handsomely for the right to bet that individual names would move more than the index. It worked, until it didn't. Over the last three weeks the spread has compressed by about 40 percent. Dispersion books that were up big in 2025 are giving back, and the unwind is itself a vol-suppressant: people closing dispersion trades sell single-stock vol and buy index vol, which puts a steady bid under the VIX-shaped instruments and a steady offer under the single-name ones.

Two: corporate buybacks are back at the post-earnings cadence

Buyback blackout windows ended for the bulk of the S&P 500 in the first week of February. The aggregate authorisation through Q1 is now north of $260bn — not a record, but firmly in the top quartile. Buybacks are mechanically vol-suppressive because corporates are price-insensitive buyers below their target VWAP, so they show up as flow exactly when natural sellers would otherwise widen the bid-ask. The first half of February historically prints lower realised vol than any other two-week window of the year, and that's not random.

Three: the carry trade hasn't broken

Despite a lot of breathless commentary in January about the yen carry unwinding, the actual position carry — measured by FX vol-adjusted returns — is still positive. Until that turns negative, you don't get the kind of forced deleveraging that produces a vol shock. Watch USD/JPY 1-month implied versus 1-month realised: when implied flips above realised by more than 0.5 vol points, that's the signal worth respecting. Right now it's flat.

What I'm doing

I'm not buying VIX. I'm not buying VIX calls. I'm not even buying VIX call spreads. What I am doing is moving incremental new capital into shorter-dated credit (six-month investment grade) and trimming the multi-year duration trades I put on in November when the curve was 80bps cheaper. The vol regime says "calmer for longer" and the credit market is consistent with that.

The risk to this view is not a black swan. It's a Fed that mis-communicates the March SEP. If you want to hedge, hedge that.
